answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: Disorderly scenario / Too Little Too Late, >2°C (RCP 4.5)
IEA Net Zero Emissions Scenario (NZE) – 1.5°C by 2050
answers.evidence: Scenario: • Orderly scenario, 1.5°C (RCP 2.6) • Disorderly scenario, 2°C (RCP 4.5) • Hot house world scenario, 3+°C (RCP 8.5) ... The modelling analysis used low-emissions climate scenarios based on the International Energy Agency's (IEA's) Net Zero 2050 Scenario (1.5ºC by 2050) and the Announced Pledges Scenario (1.8ºC by 2050)
answers.location_hint: Table 2, p.10; Table 4, p.44; Appendix A, p.29
answers.notes: The first three scenarios (Orderly, Disorderly, Hothouse) are used for the main CCSA process. The IEA NZE and APS scenarios are used specifically for the investment portfolio deep-dive analysis in FP25.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C (Orderly/RCP 2.6), >2°C (Disorderly/RCP 4.5), and >3°C (Hothouse/RCP 8.5). For the IEA investment scenarios: 1.5°C by 2050 (NZE) and 1.8°C by 2050 (APS). Temperature outcomes are referenced against IPCC Fifth Assessment Report expected ranges by 2050, not explicitly by 2100.
answers.evidence: Orderly scenario, 1.5°C (RCP 2.6) • Disorderly scenario, 2°C (RCP 4.5) • Hot house world scenario, 3+°C (RCP 8.5) ... Temperature outcomes referenced here are based on IPCC Fifth Assessment Report expected temperature ranges by 2050.
answers.notes: The document explicitly states temperature outcomes are based on IPCC AR5 expected ranges by 2050, not 2100. No explicit 2100 temperature targets are stated.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: The Financial Services Council (FSC) is the primary scenario provider for the main CCSA scenarios. The International Energy Agency (IEA) is the provider for the investment portfolio deep-dive scenarios.
answers.evidence: As a member of the Financial Services Council (FSC), we were part of a working group in FY23 that developed climate scenarios for the life insurance sector. The resulting climate scenarios aided our own scenario analysis... The climate scenarios and narratives we referred to in our analysis were based on the Climate Scenario Narratives for the Financial Services Sector (June 2023)... The modelling analysis used low-emissions climate scenarios based on the International Energy Agency's (IEA's) Net Zero 2050 Scenario (1.5ºC by 2050) and the Announced Pledges Scenario (1.8ºC by 2050)
answers.notes: FSC = Financial Services Council (New Zealand). The document also references IPCC AR5 for temperature calibration.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Orderly scenario, 1.5°C (RCP 2.6) • Disorderly scenario, 2°C (RCP 4.5) • Hot house world scenario, 3+°C (RCP 8.5 / RCP 6.0)
answers.notes: The document only uses RCP identifiers (RCP 2.6, RCP 4.5, RCP 6.0, RCP 8.5). No SSP identifiers are mentioned anywhere in the document. Therefore there is no mixing of SSP and RCP families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: As a member of the Financial Services Council (FSC), we were part of a working group in FY23 that developed climate scenarios for the life insurance sector. The resulting climate scenarios aided our own scenario analysis... In our view the FSC scenarios were relevant and appropriate for Asteron's use as they were developed specifically for the life insurance sector.
answers.notes: The scenarios were developed specifically for the life insurance/financial services sector by the FSC working group (of which Asteron Life was a member), indicating industry-level customisation. However, the document does not use explicit terms like "downscaled" or "localised". The customisation is at the industry/sector level rather than geographic localisation.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: As a member of the Financial Services Council (FSC), we were part of a working group in FY23 that developed climate scenarios for the life insurance sector.
answers.notes: Asteron Life participated in an FSC working group that collectively developed the climate scenarios for the life insurance sector. This constitutes participatory/collaborative scenario development, though the document does not use the specific keywords "co-created", "participatory", or "co-design". The working group nature of the development implies collaborative input from multiple sector participants.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: The baseline year forms the starting point from which we need to reduce our emissions by at least 90% over a specific period to reach a state of net-zero.
answers.notes: The document defines "baseline" only in the context of GHG emissions measurement (a starting year for emissions reduction tracking), not as a reference scenario against which climate scenarios are compared. No business-as-usual or reference scenario is described for the CCSA.

answers.question: Model used
answers.answer: No specific named climate models or IAMs are identified. External consultants/tools referenced include S&P Trucost and Baringa (for investment portfolio analysis). No model names such as CLIMADA, REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM, etc. are mentioned.
answers.evidence: Asteron Life engaged S&P Trucost and Baringa to support its analysis... External consultants Baringa, EY, PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC), Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu and Green Moves provided the required support for our climate disclosures including greenhouse gas emissions calculations, reporting and assurance. EY was engaged to develop the climate change scenario analysis process.
answers.notes: The document names consulting firms (S&P Trucost, Baringa, EY, PwC, Deloitte, Aon, Green Moves) but does not name any specific climate models or integrated assessment models used in the analysis.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Risk of physical climate changes including continued increase in frequency and/or severity of extreme weather events impacting insurance underwriting and reinsurance risks... In the first half of the 21st century this physical risk sees increasing severity of extreme weather which is accompanied by rising sea levels in the latter half of the 21st century... Scope of assessment: Extreme heat... Metrics considered: Expected average annual mortalities due to extreme heat
answers.location_hint: Table 4, p.44; Table 7, p.20; Scenario descriptions p.28/45
answers.notes: The document explicitly considers acute physical risks (extreme weather events, extreme heat) and quantifies expected average annual mortalities due to extreme heat. However, the analysis is primarily qualitative for transition risks, and the physical risk analysis focuses on mortality/health impacts rather than direct property damage or financial loss estimates from extreme events. The scenario analysis does acknowledge acute physical risks as a category.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: extreme weather / storm frequency and severity
answers.evidence: This is matched by an increasing carbon price that reinforces low carbon behaviour change... rising sea levels in the latter half of the 21st century... increasing severity of extreme weather... With carbon prices rising towards $140/tCO₂-e by 2040, fossil fuel–linked sectors... Technologies to decarbonise hard-to-abate sectors... Behavioural shifts, such as accelerated uptake of EVs and reduced air travel demand... The share of renewables in global electricity supply rises from 27% in 2019 to 60% in 2030 in the NZE2050... Expected average annual mortalities due to extreme heat
answers.notes: Variables are drawn from both the FSC scenario narratives and the IEA scenario sector trend assumptions.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: The Orderly scenario represents collective action towards a low carbon global economy... The impact of these activities continues to drive emissions higher throughout the remaining 21st century leading to significant materialisation of acute and chronic physical risks. In the first half of the 21st century this physical risk sees increasing severity of extreme weather which is accompanied by rising sea levels in the latter half of the 21st century. This threatens coastal developments worldwide, placing pressure on global relations.
answers.notes: The scenarios are described in global terms throughout. There is no mention of downscaling to a regional or local (New Zealand-specific) level for the scenario analysis itself, though the business context is New Zealand.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Physical Risk timeframes: Short: 2025; Medium: 2026–2040; Long: 2040–2060; Very long: 2080–2100. Transitional Risk timeframes: Short: 2025; Medium: 2026–2035; Long: 2036–2050. Rationales are explicitly provided for each timeframe.
answers.evidence: Short: 2025 (aligned with business planning, funding and operational risk timeframes) • Medium: 2026-2040 (aligned with timeframes for net-zero targets and strategic risks) • Long: 2040-2060 (aligned with NZ Government stated ambition to be net-zero by 2050) • Very long: 2080-2100 (provides longer term perspective of physical risk changes) [Physical Risk] ... Short: 2025 (aligned with business planning, funding and operational risk timeframes) • Medium: 2026-2035 (aligned with timeframes for net-zero targets and strategic risks) • Long: 2036-2050 (aligned with NZ Government stated ambition to be net-zero by 2050) [Transitional Risk]
answers.notes: Rationales are clearly stated for each timeframe: business planning alignment, net-zero target alignment, NZ Government net-zero ambition (2050), and longer-term physical risk perspective (2080–2100). Physical and transitional risk timeframes differ slightly.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: In our view the FSC scenarios were relevant and appropriate for Asteron's use as they were developed specifically for the life insurance sector... These scenarios cover a diverse range of climate scenarios to stress test against high transition risk and high physical risk scenarios respectively.
answers.notes: Two justifications are provided: (1) FSC scenarios were chosen because they were developed specifically for the life insurance sector; (2) IEA NZE and APS scenarios were chosen because they "cover a diverse range of climate scenarios to stress test against high transition risk and high physical risk scenarios respectively."
